speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. The survivor is Ruth Keller. The interviewer is Louise [INAUDIBLE]. And the date is March 13, 1995. Ruth, we left off, on the first tape, with you mentioning that, while you had non-Jewish friends, there was another side to the coin. And you were trying to cross a park. Could you just go back now and explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The park was really sort of a churchyard. But it was a park, with benches and pretty plantings. And the children from the school would always taunt me and say-- --little ditties, which I remember, in German, and-- that I was not allowed. They had convinced me I was not allowed to go through that park because I had killed Christ. I had killed Jesus. And this was a churchyard. And no Jews could step foot there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we just walked. I walked. Didn't take the shortcut. Took the long cut. Do you remember the little ditty that you could say in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jude, Jude, [GERMAN], something like that. Jew, spit in your hat. Or all kinds. I don't really remember. And then, of course, they were much nastier and threatening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I think it was like getting up in the morning and brushing my teeth. That was part of the-- part of the scene. I don't remember it being particularly one way or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to notice things were really starting to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- maybe, I can't remember it was '37 or '38. At times, my father would bring home a German refugee, for Friday dinner, for Shabbos meal. It would usually be a single man-- either he had left his family or it was here-- who had come, for some reason or other, from Germany, and who would tell the stories of the racial laws, the application of the racial laws, and the situation in Germany. And not that I can remember anyone in particular, or any story in particular. But I can remember the atmosphere of fear, of real, palpable fear that that we'd leave, and then the discussion of what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's inevitable. It's inevitable. It's going to happen any day. And what are we going to do? And then the renewed push of Palestine, and, again, Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, strangely enough, not really the United States. Because my cousin-- my mother's oldest brother-- had come in 1933, and was very unhappy. He was very unhappy here. He could not find a-- --proper job. And I guess he would come home and make descriptions which were of dire-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if it was so much poverty, so much as-- as distress of life in America. My father had a lot of relatives here, because his father-- I think his father and his grandfather, had come to this country, I guess, at the turn of the century, had been to Chicago and back, buying hides, I believe. That's how the story goes. And so he had an aunt. My father had an aunt-- my father had cousins here-- who, eventually, became quite well-to-do. And I will come to that. But there was this feeling of life in America was really not any better than life in Austria. That is what I remember. And they did not have the foresight or the whatever it was to try to come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents talk about their fears to you? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know that they talked about them to me. But they certainly talked about them openly, for me to-- they certainly talked about them openly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. And how was that for you? Hearing about all these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very sad. Very fearful. I was very fearful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was very fearful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you remember about the day that the Nazis came in to Vienna? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- which is March 13th, which is today. It was a Saturday. [LAUGHS] And that's how I remember it. And I remember my father coming home the Friday before that. And coming in, forgetting that I understood some Hebrew, saying to my mother, [HEBREW]. Which means no good. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 19--?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember hearing-- we were in our apartment-- doors locked-- the shouting. And I remember-- I told you I lived not far from a main thoroughfare-- the shouting, band music, and a general celebration and the wringing of hands, wringing of hands. And hearing, from all the radios, Hitler's voice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then things kind of became very confused. I mean, for me, terribly confused.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they confiscated Jewish businesses. They-- the-- the stories begin, the true stories of the Jews being taken in streets and having to scrub the-- the sidewalks. Of course, the identifiable Jews, the ones with beards and so on, and the fear of really walking on the street. And mine having to leave that school. Because no Jews-- all Jews were thrown out of public school, and having to go to a Jewish school far away. I had to take-- I can't even remember. I had to take the trolley. And I was constantly worried when I was not with my mother and father. Constant-- I remember that, constant worry. When I was with them, I didn't care. I didn't care what they talked about. I really didn't even listen, as long as I had both of them together. And it got to the point where I went to the store with my father. Because, of course, that summer we didn't go anyplace. I went to the store with my father. Because I felt, he's the one who has to go out on the street. And he'll be the one who will never come back. So I would go with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in-- up-- in what grade in school, when you stopped sort of going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fourth grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fourth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's store? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was some kind-- rather than having it totally confiscated, the Judische Kultusgemeinde, which was the community in Vienna, we paid taxes to, the Jewish community office, it still exists. It's a semiofficial organization. And it was-- how to explain this? Oh, it would take too long. It was-- you paid taxes. And they were a Jewish welfare agency, or something like that. And I believe that my-- they suggested that they liquidate the store to them-- at, of course, a ridiculous-- because some-- they put in Aryan directors, Aryan bosses. So that some-- They lost it. The bottom line is, they lost-- they lost the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your life began to change. You were-- did you have to wear anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --yet? Signifying you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. However, I, on one of those trips, I remember going, where my father had given me a pair of black shoes with a buckle on the side. And it had red trim. That was the fashion of the day. And track backwards. We had a super in that house who was Czech-- from Czechoslovakia. Very poor, many children. And every Christmas, my father gave them shoes or slippers or something, which is what they used all year long. And one day I came back. I had to cross a bridge. There is the canal, the Danube canal. So and I was crossing that bridge by myself. After all, I was 10 and 1/2. And I came across her. She came to me and she said to me, Jews are not allowed to wear these shoes. These are only shoes for Christian children. Take them off. And I remember holding on to the parapet of the bridge. And she took my shoes, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is 'she'? The-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the super.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the-- the super's--

speaker: interviewee
text: The super.

speaker: interviewer
text: The super.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And walking, in my socks, quite a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember how that must have felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember how that felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ashamed. I was ashamed that I was walking without shoes. I was crying. I was ashamed that I was crying. And I really was confused. That's all I remember. But mainly, the shame. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is how things changed. And I was confused. And then came the Friday night of Kristallnacht. Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The 9th of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the year--

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you remember, Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the rumors. There must have been rumors, saying that an action was about to happen. Because my father said, I am not safe at home. I'm definitely not safe at home. And they were deliberating. And a brother-in-law of my father's had a-- a kind of a-- a loft. He was in the business of, um-- I don't know what you call it here-- fabric leftovers. When tailors and big manufacturers cut clothes, they are these, um-- that they shave-- they are the leftover, which are then reprocessed and made into blankets or whatever. And he was in that kind of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a big kind of loft, where there were workers who would sort wool from cotton, from silk. And it was a-- I loved to go there, because it was full of fabric and a big mess.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- that was located in the Jewish district, but in the back of an apartment house. You had to go through a courtyard. And it had, I remember, glass-- lots of windows on top. And they decided-- and another couple of men, who they were, I don't remember-- to hide in these fabric remnants overnight. So my father went with my brother-in-law. They left. And my mother, of course, was terribly scared to be in the apartment. So we went to her sister's house, which was in the second district, on Rembrandtgasse. And we were then, the two sisters and my little cousin, who-- who was six years younger than I. And they were practically sitting at the window, all night long, watching what was going on. And we heard. We heard the running-- the running and the steps in that building. Because there, there were many Jews in that building, where my aunt lived. And the knocking at the door, and the opening of the door, and the walking in and just looking around. And my aunt saying, well, I don't know where they are. You must have taken them already. I don't know where my husband is. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to the door? Who knocked on the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were Austrians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They looked-- they were in uniform. And the fear was beyond description. Because they wore boots. Their-- their steps were heavy. That knock on the door, it took me years to overcome this, when somebody would knock at the door. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the next morning it was all over. And my-- my mother, my aunt said, well, we have to get them out now. Because they had locked themselves in. And when we got to that loft, what they had done, they had put a seal on the entrances to Jewish shops. So, on the lock, there was a seal. And my aunt and my mother, I guess, and others, were afraid to break that seal. So they communicated, you know? They talked to each other through the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there. I was present. Because I was kind of a shield, in those days. They-- I suppose they didn't do too much damage, yet, with children around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they threw some rocks. And they climbed out, out of one of those broken windows. Now, that was possible because they were surrounded by Jews. You know, there were no-- and that is how they came out. And from then on, it was really a what do we do now? Because my parents had not registered with the American Consulate on time to-- to get a visa. You know, it was the quota system. Their quota was just backed up. And not having any money to buy ourselves a visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some visas to be had to strange places, which one had never heard of. For instance, the Dominican Republic, Shanghai. Those things were very costly. We didn't have that kind of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the push came, the writing to America, do something, you know, whatever it was. And my father feared that Switzerland would be the next country to go. Which, of course, was poor judgment. But who knew at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were relatives in Belgium. And I think the thrust was to try and get to Belgium. There, too, you know, you had to buy your way in. It was illegal. Friend-- Nobody let any Jews in. I mean, I want to reiterate, I'm sure this is a known fact, there was no restrictions on the part of Nazi Germany, of Austria, of letting people out. That was their declared policy. You may leave. Nobody was prevented from leaving. As long as you left-- left with 10 deutsche mark and nothing else. You were allowed to leave. And of course, as we know, nobody wanted us. So there was nowhere to go. And that is really, essentially, the story of destruction of the European Jews. They had no place to go. So, sometime in December--

speaker: interviewee
text: '38. A knock-- that famous knock, late at night, came at the door. And two little policemen, Austrian policemen, came to get my father. And my father had been-- had fought in the war and was left with malaria, in the Italian campaign. He was-- he put the telephone wires-- he was a communications whatever. So in the swamps, in some swamps in Italy, he got malaria. And every year in August, I remember, an attack, which was then came under control with quinine. And he had a little booklet which had declared him a-- a disabled war veteran. And he showed these kids this booklet. He said, you can't arrest me. I-- I'm a war veteran. And I remember one of them just sort of knocking it out of his hand and saying, it's because of you filthy Jews that we poor Austrians lost the war. Get going. And they took him away. And, of course, my mother and then a cousin of-- an older cousin of mine went to the police station. And it just so happened he was released because the desk sergeant recognized him. And he went through the back. And he came home, which was a Saturday morning. And he said, I must leave now. Now. And he called his brother-in-law. And they agreed to go. And they packed a little suitcase. And by that time, I think, it was Sunday. Because we went to the railroad station and saw them off. And from the railroad station, we went-- my mother and her sister went to the cemetery. And I said goodbye to my father. And I just was sure that was it. I would never see him again. And we went, then, to the cemetery. And my little cousin and I were told to wait, a little bit, outside. Because we were not according to traditional Jewish law, you know, children are not supposed to go to the grave. And my mother and her sister went to pray on-- on their parents' graves. And then I-- we were frozen. It was so cold. And my little cousin was just shivering. And so was I. And my aunt said, we'll buy you a hot chocolate. There was this big cafe on the corner, where the trolley car stopped at the cemetery. It was a few steps up, three steps up. And there was a big sign saying, 'Dogs and Jews, entrance forbidden.' And I remember my mother saying, look at this, and just remember this. So we took the trolley back. We were frozen. And then there were, I guess, letters and telegrams, back and forth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father? Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went to Belgium. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was successful in-- you see, you got to the German frontier, and to the border guards. And you told the border guards, we are leaving for-- we're going to Belgium. They searched you, to make sure you had no more than 10 marks. They were, I remember, courteous. And that's it! Those were the Germans! That's it. And then, though, you had to have a-- a guide to bring your way into Belgium. Because the Belgian would reject you at the border. They rejected the Jews at the border. And it was very bad, if you were rejected, to get back into their hands, then. I understand you-- you were then put into-- in those days, they didn't have the death camps yet, but the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father and his brother-in-law and a relative of his brother-in-law got to Antwerp successfully, where my father had relatives who were nice people. Very nice people, who had gone-- whose parents had gone from Poland to Antwerp, and who had done well. So then they sent my mother and her sister all the necessary guidelines to-- to join them. And we left by train, later that December, to Aachen, in Germany, which was the Belgian border. How was that for you, leaving your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember leaving with-- with-- with-- with great anticipation and being glad to leave. Because I'm going to see my father. Being very glad to go away, very, very glad. Not a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your feelings during the time that your father was away, how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remember it as a nightmare. A 24-hour-successive nightmare. Because although, of course, my mother, at that time, reassured me, and my aunt, they were safe. They were in Belgium. I wasn't with them. So it was a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] nightmarish time. And, in the meantime, the background was more and more persecution on the streets. This one got caught on the street. That one got caught on the street. So that-- that was the kind of background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody personally that was getting caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. An uncle of mine got caught. But that was after I left. After I left. No, I can't remember [? nothing ?] personal. And then we also made it, that way, into Belgium. On the 1st of January, 1939, New Year's Eve.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, entering Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: We entered in an open-- it was an open truck. And we had to lie on the floor of an open truck. Lie on the floor of the open truck. And we were covered with some light merchandise. So because those were paid-- those were  the guides, les passeurs, who were paid to, uh-- And then we-- I remember driving, driving sort of all night, until dawn, and then getting to the cousin's apartment, and finding my father and my uncle. And a real bed, a warm bed, and a people with a different-- with totally different attitude. Of course, this was free Belgium. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Overwhelming, I imagine. Overwhelming. On the one hand, glad to be with my father. On the other hand, of course, both my mother and my father had tremendous worries about those left behind. They left a sister behind and other relatives. And it was a constant state of tension.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do? You were tense. And you felt persecuted because, in fact, you were persecuted. Do you remember you-- the truck ride that you took, how that felt, lying on the truck, covered by--

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared. I remember the German-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --guards. I remember, for instance, I think, with the very little money that my parents had, what they did was buy a little jewelry. Because and then, I remember, my mother used Nivea cream, those navy-blue boxes. And I remember, she put a ring on the bottom and then smoothed the top and closed the-- and that's how she got-- And then she put a couple of the things in the pads of my coat, in the shoulder pads of my coat. And I was very scared about that. And a few things like that. Because we weren't thoroughly searched. But I remember the Germans who-- who searched us being absolutely sort of matter-of-fact. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- the-- the fear started, because it was very dark. It was a dark night, a totally dark night, and it being very dark. And then taking one truck and then another truck. And then there was one moment where I thought I would be separated, where they would take the children. There was talk about my little cousin and me being on a different truck from my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I said, absolutely, I don't care. No. I remember saying no, absolutely not. So that was rearranged. And we were with our mother. Yeah, we were covered with a-- now that I'm thinking about-- kind of a tarp. And once we were in, they took the tarp off. And it was-- well, of course, dawn must have come very late. We're talking January 1st. But I remember the-- the roads, which I then, of course, got to know in France, too. So straight and planted with either poplar trees or some very neat kind of roads, very deserted. But very flat and very un-Austrian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was in January 1939, when we got to-- to Antwerp. The problem in Antwerp was, there was no money. No means of-- no means of support. There were laws. You couldn't work. You could not get work papers for anything at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my uncle, with his-- these fabric things, you know, that he had, my uncle had a relative there. So he got to work there. And all in a clandestine matter. And my father, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, at that point, began to sew for other people. And she had a rich cousin who had a beautiful house in Antwerp. Marble steps, the works. Crystal chandeliers. And they gave us-- they gave us an apartment there. They-- we had two rooms in the attic, which were the servant quarters. So my parents had one room, I had the other. And then the kitchen was in the-- in the basement. And it was my job to keep the-- the door knobs and all the whatever it was, copper or whatever, shining. So once a week, I was shining that stuff. I-- I hated it. But I had a compensation. Because she had a backyard. And that's what I loved. So I planted string beans. I remember I planted string beans. And I planted little flowers in her backyard. She was a forbidding person, that cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was relative of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: --mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And of course, my mother then entered me in a school. And it was-- that was another nightmare. Of course, Flemish was out. French was out. The-- it must have been a very good school. Because we're talking 1939, and this was a bilingual-- a bilingual school, believe it or not.
